feynman.0: Keep a coil turning steadily in a steady magnetic field and the flux threading it never stops changing — so an emf gets induced for just as long as you keep cranking. That’s the whole trick. The only thing left to decide is how you wire the spinning coil to the outside world: slip rings hand you AC; a split-ring commutator hands you DC.
feynman.1: A generator is nothing fancier than a coil of N turns sitting on an axle between the poles of a magnet, with something turning it — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, or just your hand on a crank. It’s the simple DC motor from Area of Study 2, only with the energy running the other way: a motor takes electrical energy and gives you rotation; a generator takes rotation and gives you electrical energy. Same machine, flipped. Figure 7.1 shows the two standard forms.
feynman.2: As the coil spins, the flux through it is forever changing. It’s biggest when the plane of the coil is perpendicular to the field, so the coil shows its full face to the field lines; it drops to zero when the plane is parallel to the field and not a single line threads through. In the maximum-flux position:
feynman.3: Spin it steadily and the coil glides between these two orientations, so the flux swings up and down like a sine wave, between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. And Faraday’s law takes that changing flux and turns it into an emf:
feynman.4: Here’s the part that catches people. The emf at any instant isn’t set by how big the flux is — it’s set by the gradient of the flux–time graph, by how fast the flux is changing. So two spots in every turn do exactly the opposite of what you’d first guess from the size of the flux. When the flux is at its maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — for that instant the flux isn’t changing at all: the gradient is zero, so the emf is zero. And when the flux is zero — coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing fastest of all, because the field lines are just about to quit threading one face and start threading the other: the gradient is steepest, so the emf is at its peak.
feynman.5: So the emf trace comes out as a sine wave with the same period as the flux, but shoved over by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak sits right under a flux zero, and every emf zero right under a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 lines up one full turn against both graphs so you can see it.
feynman.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε is biggest where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is at a maximum and, for that instant, not changing at all.
feynman.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” gets it exactly backwards. At the maximum-flux position the coil is showing its full face to the field, sure, but for that instant the flux isn’t changing, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero right then. What makes an emf is the flux changing — not the flux being large.
feynman.8: Magnus hand-cranks a generator whose rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, sitting in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Find the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil turns from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
feynman.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
feynman.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, and a quarter turn later it’s fallen to zero, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
feynman.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes only Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — watch out, not the full period.
feynman.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. Remember, that’s an average over the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf is changing the whole way round, peaking right at the zero-flux instant.
feynman.13: Connects This is the very same coil-in-a-field setup as the simple DC motor, just run backwards. In Area of Study 2 you put a current in the field and got rotation out, through F = nIlB; here you put rotation in the field and get an emf out, through Faraday’s law — one machine, two directions for the energy to flow.
feynman.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the spinning coil joins onto its own unbroken metal ring — a slip ring — riding on the axle, and a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, as in Figure 7.1(a). The slip rings keep the electrical contact going between the turning coil and the stationary outside circuit, so you can draw current off the coil without the wires winding themselves into a knot.
feynman.15: Because each end of the coil keeps its own ring forever, the connections never swap over, and the terminals just follow the coil’s emf faithfully: when the emf flips every half turn, the output flips right along with it, and the alternator gives you the sine wave untouched — alternating current. Just about all the electricity on the grid is made this way, by turbine-driven alternators spinning at a rate locked to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. On any diagram the giveaway is just the count: two separate rings means slip rings, which means AC output.
feynman.16: Now swap those two slip rings for a single ring sliced into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half wired to one end of the coil — and the very same machine gives you DC, as in Figure 7.1(b). Every half turn the brushes cross the gaps and jump from one half-ring to the other, timed dead-on to the moment the coil passes the maximum-flux position — the instant the emf is zero and just about to reverse. So each time the coil’s emf flips, the connection flips with it, and the polarity at the output terminals never changes: the negative half-cycles of the sine wave get folded upward, as in Figure 7.2.
feynman.17: The output is direct current, all right, but it’s far from steady: it climbs from zero up to the peak and drops back twice every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — it forces it to keep one sign — but it does nothing whatever to smooth it out.
feynman.18: That same split-ring commutator shows up in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, doing the opposite job: in the motor it flips the supply current through the coil every half turn so the torque always pushes the same way round; in the generator it flips the coil’s connection to the circuit so the output always keeps one polarity. And don’t forget — a generator’s rotation comes from the external torque, not from its commutator.
feynman.19: Key idea One part, two jobs: the split-ring commutator reverses the current through a motor’s coil, and rectifies the output of a generator. Slip rings keep AC as it is; a split ring gives you varying DC.
feynman.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not give you a constant voltage. Its output is still swinging the whole time between zero and the peak, twice every revolution — it’s DC only in the sense that the sign never flips over. And calling the commutator’s job in a generator “keeping the coil turning” drags the motor explanation into the wrong machine.
feynman.21: Put an oscilloscope across the output terminals and it draws you the emf–time trace, and four quantities describe what you see. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the greatest value the emf reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full height from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest, so Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sine wave. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — and for a single rotating coil, that’s exactly one revolution. The frequency f is just how many cycles you get each second:
feynman.22: When the alternator pushes a current, the current alternates right in step with the emf, and you read it the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 lines up one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs, with the trace measurements marked. Reading it off is straightforward: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with one crest to the next 40 ms apart, give you an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — so the coil turns 25 times every second.
feynman.23: Now spin the coil twice as fast and watch what happens — two things change together. Each revolution takes half as long, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. And at the same time, every flux change is now crammed into half the time: ΔΦB is the same as before while Δt is halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf doubles at every point on the trace — the amplitude jumps from 6.0 V to 12 V and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
feynman.24: Reading it the other way round matters just as much: if you see a trace whose amplitude has doubled but the period hasn’t budged, that can’t be from faster spinning — faster spinning would have shortened the period too. An amplitude-only change has to come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A — each of those scales the flux swing without touching the timing.
feynman.25: Key idea The spin rate sets both the timing and the size of the output: double it and you double both the frequency and the amplitude. An amplitude change with the frequency unchanged has to come from N, B or A instead.
feynman.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell makes electricity with no moving parts at all: it turns light energy straight into electrical energy. No coil, no magnet, no changing flux anywhere. Light soaked up in thin layers of treated semiconductor knocks electrons loose, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers herds them around the outside circuit in one direction only: the output is DC for as long as light keeps falling on the cell. One cell on its own gives only a small voltage, so a solar panel wires many cells together, and a rooftop array wires many panels together.
feynman.27: Exactly how the layers set up that junction field is semiconductor physics that lives beyond this course, and the inverter’s internal circuitry is left out for the same reason; all you need here is what goes in and what comes out of each device.
feynman.28: Households run on the grid’s standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz — alternating, because the grid was built around alternators in the first place, and because transformers only work on AC. So a rooftop solar system feeds its panels into an inverter, the gadget that converts the panels’ DC into mains-compatible AC, which can then run the household’s appliances or be sent back out to the grid.
feynman.29: Connects Light can knock electrons loose from a material because it delivers its energy in discrete packets — photons, as we treat in Unit 4. The photoelectric effect is the lab demonstration of the very physics that every PV cell relies on.

sagan.0: Turn a coil steadily within a constant magnetic field, and the flux threading it never settles — it changes for as long as the coil turns, and so, for just as long, an emf is induced. Only one design choice remains: how to join that wheeling coil to the world outside. Slip rings deliver AC; a split-ring commutator delivers DC.
sagan.1: A generator is nothing more than a coil of N turns, mounted on an axle between the poles of a magnet and set turning by some external torque — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, or the simple labour of a hand crank. It is the humble DC motor of Area of Study 2, with the direction of energy conversion run backwards: where a motor takes electrical energy and gives back rotation, a generator takes rotation and gives back electrical energy. Figure 7.1 shows the two forms this machine can take.
sagan.2: As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux threading it changes from moment to moment: greatest when the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field and the coil opens its full face to the field lines, and falling to zero when the plane lies parallel to the field, where not a single line threads through. In that position of greatest flux:
sagan.3: Steady rotation carries the coil smoothly between these two extremes, so the flux rises and falls in a sinusoid, swinging between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. And Faraday’s law takes that ceaseless changing of the flux and turns it into an emf:
sagan.4: The emf at any instant is set not by the size of the flux but by the gradient of the flux–time graph — by how fast the flux is changing. So two positions in every rotation behave in exactly the opposite way to what a first glance at the flux would lead us to expect. When the flux stands at its maximum — the coil plane perpendicular to the field — the flux is, for that one instant, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, and so the emf is zero. And when the flux falls to zero — the coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing at its very fastest, for the field lines are about to abandon one face of the coil and begin threading the other: the gradient is steepest, and so the emf reaches its peak.
sagan.5: The emf trace, then, is a sinusoid sharing the flux’s period exactly, but shifted by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak rides directly beneath a flux zero, and every emf zero beneath a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 lays one full rotation against both graphs at once.
sagan.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and momentarily unchanging.
sagan.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” has the relationship turned exactly back to front. At the maximum-flux position the coil presents a full face to the field, yet for that instant the flux is not moving at all, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero. An emf is born from the flux changing — never from the flux merely being large.
sagan.8: Magnus hand-cranks a generator whose rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Calculate the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil rotates from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.

sagan.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, falling away to zero a quarter turn later, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
sagan.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a single quarter turn takes only Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — not the full period.
sagan.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. This is an average taken across the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf rises and falls throughout the rotation, reaching its peak at the very instant the flux passes through zero.
sagan.13: Connects This is the very same coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor, run in reverse. In Area of Study 2, a current set within the field gave us rotation through F = nIlB; here, rotation within the field gives us an emf through Faraday’s law — one machine, two directions of energy flow.
sagan.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the turning coil is joined to its own unbroken ring of metal — a slip ring — riding on the axle, while a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, as shown in Figure 7.1(a). These slip rings keep unbroken electrical contact between the spinning coil and the still external circuit, so that current may be drawn from the coil endlessly, without the connecting wires ever twisting themselves into knots.
sagan.15: Because each end of the coil keeps its own ring forever, the connections never swap, and the terminals follow the coil’s emf faithfully, instant by instant: when the emf reverses every half revolution, the output reverses right along with it, and the alternator delivers the sinusoid untouched — alternating current. sagan.16: Now replace those two slip rings with a single ring split into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half wired to one end of the coil — and the very same machine now delivers DC, as shown in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and leap from one half-ring to the other, timed with exquisite precision to the moment the coil passes through the maximum-flux position — the very instant the emf falls to zero and stands poised to reverse. Each time the coil’s emf reverses, the connections reverse along with it, so the polarity at the output terminals never wavers: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid are flipped upward, as shown in Figure 7.2.
sagan.17: The output is direct current, yet it is far from steady: it climbs from zero to its peak and falls back again twice in every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — compels it to keep one sign — but does nothing whatever to smooth it.

And to describe the commutator’s job in a generator as “keeping the coil turning” is to carry the motor’s explanation across into the wrong machine entirely.

curie.2: As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux through it changes continuously. It is greatest when the plane of the coil is perpendicular to the field and the coil presents its full area to the field lines, and zero when the plane is parallel to the field and no field lines thread the coil. In the maximum-flux position:
curie.3: Uniform rotation carries the coil smoothly between these orientations, so the flux rises and falls sinusoidally between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. Faraday’s law turns that changing flux into an emf:
curie.4: The emf at any instant is set by the gradient of the flux–time graph, not by the size of the flux. Two positions in each rotation therefore behave in the opposite way to a first expectation based on the size of the flux. When the flux is at a maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — the flux is, for that instant, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, so the emf is zero. When the flux is zero — coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing at its fastest, because the field lines are about to stop threading one face of the coil and start threading the other: the gradient is steepest, so the emf is at its peak.
curie.5: The emf trace is therefore a sinusoid with the same period as the flux but shifted by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak sits directly below a flux zero, and every emf zero below a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 sets one full rotation against both graphs.
curie.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and momentarily unchanging.
curie.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” states the relationship the wrong way around. At the maximum-flux position the coil presents a full but momentarily unchanging area to the field, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero at that instant. An emf requires the flux to be changing — it does not require the flux to be large.
curie.8: Magnus hand-cranks a generator whose rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Calculate the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil rotates from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
curie.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
curie.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, falling to zero a quarter turn later, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
curie.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — not the full period.
curie.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. This is an average taken over the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf varies through the rotation, peaking at the zero-flux instant.
curie.13: Connects This is the coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor run in reverse. In Area of Study 2 a current placed in the field produced rotation via F = nIlB; here rotation in the field produces an emf via Faraday’s law — one machine, two directions of energy flow.
curie.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the rotating coil is joined to its own continuous metal ring — a slip ring — mounted on the axle, and a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, as shown in Figure 7.1(a). curie.15: Because each coil end keeps its own ring permanently, the connections never swap, and the terminals follow the coil’s emf exactly: when the emf reverses every half revolution, the output reverses with it, and the alternator delivers the unaltered sinusoid — alternating current. curie.16: Replace the two slip rings with a single ring split into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half connected to one end of the coil — and the same machine delivers DC, as shown in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and swap from one half-ring to the other, timed exactly to the coil passing the maximum-flux position — the instant the emf is zero and about to reverse direction. curie.17: The output is direct current, but it is not steady: it rises from zero to the peak value and falls back twice per revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — forces it to keep one sign — but does nothing to smooth it.
curie.18: The same split-ring commutator appears in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, doing the opposite job: in the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution so the torque keeps one rotational direction; in the generator it reverses the coil’s connection to the circuit so the output keeps one polarity. curie.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not produce a constant voltage. Its output still varies continuously between zero and the peak value, twice per revolution — it is DC only in the sense that the sign never reverses. curie.21: An oscilloscope placed across the output terminals draws the emf–time trace, and four quantities describe it. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the greatest value the emf reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full vertical span from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest, so Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — for a single rotating coil, exactly one revolution. The frequency f is the number of cycles each second:
curie.22: When the alternator drives a current, the current alternates in step with the emf and is read in the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. hawking.1: A generator is a coil of N turns, mounted on an axle between the poles of a magnet and turned by some external torque — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, a hand crank. It is the simple DC motor of Area of Study 2, run backwards. A motor turns electrical energy into rotation. A generator turns rotation into electrical energy. One machine, two directions. Figure 7.1 shows the two standard forms.
hawking.2: As the coil rotates, the flux through it changes without pause. It is greatest when the plane of the coil sits perpendicular to the field and the coil shows its full area to the field lines. It is zero when the plane lies parallel to the field and no lines thread the coil at all. In the maximum-flux position:
hawking.3: Uniform rotation carries the coil smoothly between these orientations, so the flux rises and falls sinusoidally between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. Faraday’s law turns that changing flux into an emf:
hawking.4: What sets the emf at any instant is the gradient of the flux–time graph, not the size of the flux. So two positions in each turn behave the opposite way to what the size of the flux would suggest. When the flux is at a maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — the flux is, for that instant, not changing at all. The gradient is zero, so the emf is zero. When the flux is zero — coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing fastest, because the field lines are about to stop threading one face and start threading the other. The gradient is steepest, so the emf is at its peak.
hawking.5: The emf trace is therefore a sinusoid with the same period as the flux, shifted by a quarter of a cycle (90°). Every emf peak sits directly below a flux zero. Every emf zero sits below a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 sets one full rotation against both graphs.
hawking.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux. ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest. ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and, for that instant, not changing.
hawking.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” states the relationship backwards. At the maximum-flux position the coil shows a full but momentarily unchanging area to the field, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero at that instant. An emf needs the flux to be changing. It does not need the flux to be large.
hawking.8: Magnus hand-cranks a generator whose rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Calculate the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil rotates from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
hawking.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
hawking.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position. A quarter turn later it has fallen to zero, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
hawking.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — not the full period.
hawking.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. This is an average over the quarter turn. The instantaneous emf varies through the rotation, reaching its peak at the zero-flux instant.
hawking.13: Connects This is the coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor, run in reverse. In Area of Study 2 a current placed in the field produced rotation via F = nIlB. Here rotation in the field produces an emf via Faraday’s law. One machine, two directions of energy flow.
hawking.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the rotating coil is joined to its own continuous metal ring — a slip ring — mounted on the axle, and a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, as shown in Figure 7.1(a). The slip rings hold continuous electrical contact between the rotating coil and the stationary external circuit, so current can be drawn from the coil without the wires twisting.
hawking.15: Each coil end keeps its own ring for good, so the connections never swap and the terminals follow the coil’s emf exactly. When the emf reverses every half revolution, the output reverses with it. The alternator delivers the sinusoid untouched — alternating current. Almost all grid electricity is made this way, by turbine-driven alternators turning at a rate locked to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. On any diagram the recognition cue is the count: two separate rings means slip rings, and therefore AC output.
hawking.16: Replace the two slip rings with a single ring split into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half joined to one end of the coil — and the same machine delivers DC, as shown in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and swap from one half-ring to the other, timed to the instant the coil passes the maximum-flux position — the instant the emf is zero and about to reverse. Each time the coil’s emf reverses, the connections reverse with it, so the polarity at the output terminals never changes. The negative half-cycles of the sinusoid are flipped upward, as shown in Figure 7.2.
hawking.17: The output is direct current, but it is not steady. It rises from zero to the peak value and falls back twice per revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — forces it to keep one sign — but does nothing to smooth it.
hawking.18: The same split-ring commutator appears in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, doing the opposite job. In the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution, so the torque keeps one rotational direction. In the generator it reverses the coil’s connection to the circuit, so the output keeps one polarity. A generator’s rotation comes from the external torque, not from its commutator.
hawking.19: Key idea One component, two roles. The split-ring commutator reverses the current through a motor’s coil, and rectifies the output of a generator. Slip rings preserve AC. A split ring produces varying DC.
hawking.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not produce a constant voltage. Its output still varies, continuously, between zero and the peak value, twice per revolution. It is DC only in the sense that the sign never reverses. To describe the commutator’s job in a generator as “keeping the coil turning” is to import the motor explanation into the wrong machine.
hawking.21: An oscilloscope across the output terminals draws the emf–time trace, and four quantities describe it. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the greatest value the emf reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full vertical span from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest, so Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — for a single rotating coil, exactly one revolution. The frequency f is the number of cycles each second:
hawking.22: When the alternator drives a current, the current alternates in step with the emf and is read the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 sets one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs, with the trace measurements marked. The reading is direct. Crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — the coil turns 25 times each second.
hawking.23: Now drive the coil twice as fast. Two quantities change together. Each revolution takes half the time, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. At the same time, every flux change now happens in half the time: ΔΦB is unchanged while Δt is halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf doubles at every point on the trace. The amplitude rises from 6.0 V to 12 V, and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
hawking.24: The reverse reading matters just as much. A trace whose amplitude has doubled at an unchanged period cannot come from faster rotation, which would also have shortened the period. An amplitude-only change must come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A — each scales the flux swing without touching the timing.
hawking.25: Key idea Rotation speed sets both the timing and the size of the output: double it and you double both the frequency and the amplitude. An amplitude change at constant frequency must come from N, B or A instead.
hawking.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell generates electricity with no motion at all. It converts light energy directly into electrical energy. No coil, no magnet, no changing flux. Light absorbed in thin layers of treated semiconductor frees electrons, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers drives them around the external circuit in one direction only. The output is DC for as long as light falls on the cell. A single cell produces only a small voltage, so a solar panel connects many cells, and a rooftop array many panels.
hawking.27: How the layers set up that junction field is semiconductor physics beyond the scope of this course, and the inverter’s internal circuitry is excluded for the same reason. Only the input–output behaviour of each device is required.
hawking.28: Households run on the grid’s standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz. Alternating, because the grid was built around alternators, and because transformers work only on AC. A rooftop solar system therefore feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that converts the panels’ DC into mains-compatible AC, which can then power the household’s appliances or be exported to the grid.
hawking.29: Connects Light can free electrons from a material because it delivers its energy in discrete packets — photons, as treated in Unit 4. The photoelectric effect is the laboratory demonstration of the physics on which every PV cell relies.

nye.0: Here's the whole trick! Keep a coil spinning steadily inside a constant magnetic field and the flux threading through it just keeps on changing — so you get an induced emf for as long as that coil keeps turning. The only design decision left is how you hook that spinning coil up to the outside circuit: go with slip rings and you get AC; go with a split-ring commutator and you get DC.
nye.1: So what's a generator? It's a coil of N turns mounted on an axle, sitting between the poles of a magnet, and something outside has to crank it round — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, even your own hand on a crank. Here's the cool part: it's the exact same simple DC motor from Area of Study 2, just with the energy flowing the other way! A motor takes electrical energy and turns it into rotation; a generator takes rotation and turns it into electrical energy. Figure 7.1 shows you the two standard versions.
nye.2: Now watch what happens as the coil rotates: the magnetic flux through it keeps changing the whole time. It's greatest when the plane of the coil is perpendicular to the field and the coil shows its full area to those field lines, and it's zero when the plane is parallel to the field and not a single field line threads through. In that maximum-flux position:
nye.3: Spin the coil smoothly and it sails between these two orientations, so the flux rises and falls in a perfect sinusoid, swinging between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. And Faraday's law takes that changing flux and turns it into an emf:
nye.4: Here's the part that trips everybody up! The emf at any instant is set by the gradient of the flux–time graph — the steepness — not by the size of the flux. So two positions in each rotation do the exact opposite of what you'd first guess. When the flux is at a maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — the flux is, for that split second, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, so the emf is zero! And when the flux is zero — coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing at its absolute fastest, because the field lines are just about to stop threading one face and start threading the other: the gradient is steepest, so the emf hits its peak!
nye.5: So the emf trace comes out as a sinusoid with the same period as the flux, but shoved over by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every single emf peak sits right below a flux zero, and every emf zero sits right below a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 lines up one full rotation against both graphs so you can see it.
nye.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and momentarily unchanging.
nye.7: Trap Watch out — saying “maximum flux gives maximum emf” has the relationship completely backwards! At the maximum-flux position the coil shows a full but, for that instant, unchanging area to the field, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero right then. An emf needs the flux to be changing — it doesn't care whether the flux is large.
nye.8: Magnus grabs a generator and hand-cranks it — its rectangular coil has 50 turns, each one 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, sitting in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, and he keeps it spinning at a steady 10 Hz. Calculate the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil rotates from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
nye.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
nye.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, and it drops to zero a quarter turn later — so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
nye.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — careful, that's a quarter, not the full period!
nye.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. And remember, that's an average over the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf is changing the whole way round, hitting its peak right at the zero-flux instant.
nye.13: Connects Check it out — this is the very same coil-in-field setup from the simple DC motor, just run in reverse! In Area of Study 2 you put a current in the field and got rotation out, via F = nIlB; here you put rotation in the field and get an emf out, via Faraday's law — one machine, two directions of energy flow.
nye.14: An alternator is just an AC generator. Here's how it's wired: each end of the spinning coil joins onto its own continuous metal ring — a slip ring — mounted on the axle, and a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, like you see in Figure 7.1(a). Those slip rings keep up unbroken electrical contact between the spinning coil and the stationary outside circuit, so you can draw current from the coil without the connecting wires twisting themselves into a knot.
nye.15: And because each coil end hangs onto its own ring permanently, the connections never swap over, so the terminals track the coil's emf exactly: when the emf flips every half revolution, the output flips right along with it, and the alternator hands you the untouched sinusoid — alternating current! Almost all the electricity on the grid is made this way, by turbine-driven alternators spinning at a rate locked to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. And here's your spotting cue on any diagram — just count: two separate rings means slip rings, and that means AC output.
nye.16: Now swap those two slip rings for a single ring split into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, with each half wired to one end of the coil — and the very same machine hands you DC instead, like in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and jump from one half-ring to the other, timed perfectly to the moment the coil passes the maximum-flux position — the exact instant the emf is zero and about to reverse. So every time the coil's emf reverses, the connections reverse right with it, which means the polarity at the output terminals never changes: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid get flipped up the right way, like you see in Figure 7.2.
nye.17: The output is direct current — but don't think it's steady! It climbs from zero up to the peak value and falls back down twice every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — it forces it to keep one sign — but it does absolutely nothing to smooth it out.
nye.18: And get this — that exact same split-ring commutator turns up in the DC motor from Area of Study 2, but doing the opposite job! In the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution so the torque keeps pushing one rotational direction; in the generator it reverses the coil's connection to the circuit so the output keeps one polarity. And remember: a generator's rotation comes from the external torque — not from its commutator.
nye.19: Key idea One component, two roles: the split-ring commutator reverses the current through a motor's coil, and rectifies the output of a generator. Slip rings preserve AC; a split ring produces varying DC.
nye.20: Trap Don't be fooled — a split-ring commutator does not give you a constant voltage! Its output is still swinging continuously between zero and the peak value, twice every revolution — it's DC only in the sense that the sign never reverses. And saying the commutator's job in a generator is “keeping the coil turning” just drags the motor explanation into the wrong machine.
nye.21: Hook an oscilloscope across the output terminals and it draws you the emf–time trace — and there are four quantities that describe it. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the biggest value the emf ever reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full vertical span, all the way from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest, so Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — and for a single rotating coil, that's exactly one revolution. The frequency f is how many cycles you get each second:
nye.22: When the alternator drives a current, that current alternates right in step with the emf, and you read it the very same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 lines up one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs with all the trace measurements marked. Reading it off is dead simple: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give you an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — the coil turns 25 times every second!
nye.23: Now consider what happens when you crank the coil twice as fast. Two quantities change together. Each revolution now takes half the time, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. And at the same time, every flux change now happens in half the time too: ΔΦB stays the same while Δt gets halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf doubles at every single point on the trace — the amplitude jumps from 6.0 V to 12 V and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
nye.24: And reading it the other way is just as important! A trace whose amplitude has doubled while the period stayed the same can't have come from faster rotation — because faster rotation would've shortened the period too. An amplitude-only change has to come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A — each one scales the flux swing without touching the timing at all.
nye.25: Key idea Rotation speed sets both the timing and the size of the output: doubling it doubles both the frequency and the amplitude. An amplitude change at constant frequency must come from N, B or A instead.
nye.26: Now here's something totally different — a photovoltaic (PV) cell makes electricity with no motion whatsoever! It converts light energy straight into electrical energy. No coil, no magnet, no changing flux. Light gets absorbed in thin layers of treated semiconductor and frees up electrons, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers drives them around the external circuit in one direction only: you get DC for as long as light keeps falling on the cell. One cell on its own only makes a tiny voltage, so a solar panel wires up many cells, and a rooftop array wires up many panels.
nye.27: Exactly how those layers set up that junction field is semiconductor physics that's beyond the scope of this course, and the inverter's internal circuitry is left out for the same reason; all you need here is the input–output behaviour of each device.
nye.28: Your house runs on the grid's standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz — alternating, because the grid was built around alternators in the first place, and because transformers only work on AC. So a rooftop solar system feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that takes the panels' DC and converts it into mains-compatible AC, which can then power your appliances or get exported back to the grid.
nye.29: Connects Light is able to free electrons from a material because it delivers its energy in discrete packets — photons, as treated in Unit 4. The photoelectric effect is the laboratory demonstration of the physics on which every PV cell relies.

munroe.0: Spin a coil steadily inside a steady magnetic field and the flux through it never stops changing, which means an emf keeps getting induced for exactly as long as you keep turning. A split-ring commutator gives you DC. Same coil, same spin, different output, and the only difference is the connector.

bronowski.0: Turn a coil steadily in a constant magnetic field and the flux threading it never settles; it keeps changing, and so for as long as the coil turns an emf is induced. Only one design question remains—how to join the spinning coil to the world outside it. Slip rings deliver AC; a split-ring commutator delivers DC.
bronowski.1: A generator is nothing more elaborate than a coil of N turns, mounted on an axle between the poles of a magnet and driven round by some outside torque—a steam turbine, a wind turbine, or a hand at a crank. It is the simple DC motor of Area of Study 2 with the direction of energy reversed: where the motor takes electrical energy and gives back rotation, the generator takes rotation and gives back electrical energy. Figure 7.1 shows the two standard forms.
bronowski.2: As the coil turns, the magnetic flux through it never rests but changes from moment to moment—greatest when the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field and the coil offers its whole face to the field lines, and falling to zero when the plane lies parallel to the field and not one line threads it. At the position of maximum flux:
bronowski.3: Steady rotation carries the coil smoothly from one of these orientations to the other, so the flux climbs and falls sinusoidally between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. And Faraday’s law takes that changing flux and makes of it an emf:
bronowski.4: What sets the emf at any instant is not the size of the flux but the gradient of the flux–time graph—the rate at which it changes. And so two positions in every turn behave in just the opposite way to what a first glance at the flux would lead one to expect. When the flux is greatest—coil plane perpendicular to the field—it is, for that instant, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, and the emf with it. When the flux is zero—coil plane parallel to the field—it is changing faster than anywhere else, for the field lines are about to abandon one face of the coil and begin threading the other: the gradient is steepest, and the emf rises to its peak.
bronowski.5: The emf trace is therefore a sinusoid, sharing the flux’s period but shifted by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every peak of the emf falls directly beneath a zero of the flux, and every zero of the emf beneath a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 sets one full rotation against both graphs.
bronowski.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε is greatest where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB stands at a maximum and is, for that instant, unchanging.
bronowski.7: Trap To say “maximum flux gives maximum emf” is to read the relationship backwards. At the maximum-flux position the coil presents its full face to the field, yes—but that face is, for that instant, unchanging, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero. An emf asks only that the flux be changing; it never asks that the flux be large.
bronowski.8: Magnus turns a hand-crank generator whose rectangular coil carries 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, through a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Find the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil swings from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
bronowski.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full change in flux and Δt the time for one quarter of a revolution.
bronowski.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, falling to zero a quarter turn later, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
bronowski.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s—not the full period.
bronowski.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. This is an average taken over the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf rises and falls through the rotation, reaching its peak at the very instant the flux passes through zero.
bronowski.13: Connects Here is the same coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor, run in reverse. In Area of Study 2 a current set in the field gave rise to rotation through F = nIlB; here rotation in the field gives rise to an emf through Faraday’s law—one machine, and two directions in which the energy may flow.
bronowski.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the turning coil is carried to its own unbroken metal ring—a slip ring—set on the axle, and against each ring a stationary carbon brush presses, as Figure 7.1(a) shows. The slip rings hold an unbroken electrical contact between the turning coil and the stationary circuit beyond, so that current may be drawn from the coil and the connecting wires never twist.
bronowski.15: Because each end of the coil keeps its own ring for good, the connections never change places, and the terminals follow the coil’s emf faithfully: when the emf reverses, as it does every half revolution, the output reverses with it, and the alternator gives back the sinusoid untouched—alternating current. Nearly all the electricity in the grid is born this way, from turbine-driven alternators turning at a rate held fast to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. On any diagram the sign to read is the count: two separate rings means slip rings, and so AC output.
bronowski.16: Take away the two slip rings and put in their place a single ring split into two insulated halves—a split-ring commutator, each half joined to one end of the coil—and the very same machine now gives back DC, as Figure 7.1(b) shows. Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and pass from one half-ring to the other, the moment timed exactly to the coil’s passing through the maximum-flux position—the instant when the emf is zero and on the point of reversing. Each time the coil’s emf reverses, the connections reverse with it, so the polarity at the output terminals holds steady and never turns over: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid are folded upward, as Figure 7.2 shows.
bronowski.17: The output is direct current, but it is far from steady: it climbs from zero to its peak and falls back again twice in every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output—compels it to keep one sign—but it does nothing whatever to smooth it.
bronowski.18: The same split-ring commutator turns up in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, there doing the opposite work: in the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution, so the torque keeps to one direction of turning; in the generator it reverses the coil’s connection to the circuit, so the output keeps to one polarity. And a generator’s rotation is the gift of the external torque, never of its commutator.
bronowski.19: Key idea One component, two roles: the split-ring commutator reverses the current through a motor’s coil, and rectifies the output of a generator. Slip rings preserve AC; a split ring produces varying DC.
bronowski.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not give back a constant voltage. Its output still climbs and falls without rest between zero and the peak value, twice in every revolution—it is DC only in this one sense, that the sign never reverses. And to describe the commutator’s work in a generator as “keeping the coil turning” is to carry the motor’s explanation across into the wrong machine.
bronowski.21: Lay an oscilloscope across the output terminals and it draws out the emf–time trace, and four quantities describe what it draws. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the greatest value the emf ever reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full span from the floor of a trough to the summit of a crest, so that Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle—for a single turning coil, exactly one revolution. The frequency f is the count of cycles in each second:
bronowski.22: When the alternator drives a current, that current alternates in step with the emf and is read in just the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 sets one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs with the trace measurements marked. The reading is direct: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz—the coil turns 25 times in each second.
bronowski.23: Now imagine the coil driven twice as fast. Two quantities change together. Each revolution takes half the time, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. And at the same time, every change of flux is now crowded into half the time: ΔΦB is unchanged while Δt is halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf doubles at every point along the trace—the amplitude rises from 6.0 V to 12 V and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
bronowski.24: The reading run backwards matters just as much: a trace whose amplitude has doubled while its period holds unchanged cannot have come from faster rotation, which would have shortened the period as well. A change in amplitude alone must come instead from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A—each one scales the swing of the flux without ever touching the timing.
bronowski.25: Key idea Rotation speed governs both the timing and the size of the output: double it and you double both the frequency and the amplitude. A change in amplitude at constant frequency must come from N, B or A instead.
bronowski.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell makes electricity with no motion whatever: it converts the energy of light straight into electrical energy. There is no coil, no magnet, no changing flux. Light absorbed in thin layers of treated semiconductor sets electrons free, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers drives them around the external circuit in one direction only: the output is DC for as long as light falls on the cell. A single cell yields only a small voltage, so a solar panel joins many cells together, and a rooftop array many panels.
bronowski.27: How the layers come to set up that junction field is semiconductor physics that lies beyond this course, and the inner circuitry of the inverter is set aside for the same reason; what is asked of us is only the input–output behaviour of each device.
bronowski.28: Households live on the grid’s standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz—alternating, because the grid was raised around alternators, and because transformers will work on nothing but AC. A rooftop solar system therefore feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that turns the panels’ DC into mains-compatible AC, which may then power the appliances of the house or be sent out to the grid.
bronowski.29: Connects Light can free electrons from a material because it brings its energy not in a stream but in discrete packets—photons, as Unit 4 treats them. The photoelectric effect is the laboratory demonstration of the very physics on which every PV cell stands.

mack.0: Spin a coil steadily inside a constant magnetic field and the flux threading it never stops changing — so an emf keeps right on being induced for as long as the coil keeps turning. The only real design decision left is how you wire that spinning coil to the outside world: feed it through slip rings and you get AC; feed it through a split-ring commutator and you get DC.
mack.1: A generator is a coil of N turns mounted on an axle between the poles of a magnet, spun by some external torque — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, or just you cranking a handle. It is, quite literally, the simple DC motor of Area of Study 2 with the energy running the other way: a motor eats electrical energy and gives you rotation; a generator eats rotation and gives you electrical energy. Figure 7.1 shows the two standard forms.
mack.2: As the coil turns, the magnetic flux through it changes the whole time: largest when the plane of the coil sits perpendicular to the field and the coil throws its full area across the field lines, and zero when the plane lies parallel to the field and not a single field line manages to thread through. In the maximum-flux position:
mack.3: Steady rotation slides the coil smoothly between these two extremes, so the flux swells and shrinks sinusoidally between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. Faraday’s law takes that changing flux and turns it into an emf:
mack.4: Here is the part that trips people up: the emf at any instant is set by the gradient of the flux–time graph, not by how big the flux happens to be. So two positions in every rotation behave in exactly the opposite way to your first guess. When the flux is at a maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — the flux is, for that one instant, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, so the emf is zero. When the flux is zero — coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing as fast as it ever will, because the field lines are right on the verge of abandoning one face of the coil and pouring through the other: the gradient is steepest, so the emf is at its peak.
mack.5: The emf trace is therefore a sinusoid with the same period as the flux but slid along by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak sits squarely beneath a flux zero, and every emf zero beneath a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 lines up one full rotation against both graphs.
mack.6: Key idea The induced emf chases the gradient of the flux: ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and, for that instant, frozen.
mack.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” has the relationship back to front. At the maximum-flux position the coil shows a full but momentarily unchanging area to the field, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero at that very instant. An emf needs the flux to be changing — it does not care whether the flux is large.
mack.8: Magnus hand-cranks a generator whose rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Calculate the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil rotates from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
mack.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
mack.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, dropping to zero a quarter turn later, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
mack.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — not the full period, so watch that step.
mack.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. That is an average across the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf swings through the rotation, hitting its peak at the zero-flux instant.
mack.13: Connects This is the very same coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor, just run backwards. In Area of Study 2 a current dropped into the field gave you rotation via F = nIlB; here rotation in the field hands you an emf via Faraday’s law — one machine, two directions of energy flow.
mack.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the spinning coil is joined to its own unbroken metal ring — a slip ring — riding on the axle, and a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, as shown in Figure 7.1(a). The slip rings keep continuous electrical contact between the turning coil and the stationary external circuit, so you can draw current off the coil without the connecting wires winding themselves into a tangle.
mack.15: Because each coil end hangs onto its own ring forever, the connections never swap and the terminals track the coil’s emf faithfully: when the emf flips every half revolution, the output flips right along with it, and the alternator delivers the sinusoid untouched — alternating current. Nearly all grid electricity is born this way, from turbine-driven alternators spinning at a rate locked to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. On any diagram the giveaway is simply the count: two separate rings means slip rings, and therefore AC output.
mack.16: Now swap those two slip rings for a single ring sliced into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half wired to one end of the coil — and the very same machine hands you DC instead, as shown in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes leap across the gaps and jump from one half-ring to the other, timed precisely to the moment the coil sweeps past the maximum-flux position — the instant the emf is zero and just about to reverse. Each time the coil’s emf flips, the connections flip with it, so the polarity at the output terminals never budges: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid get folded back upward, as shown in Figure 7.2.
mack.17: The output is direct current, but don’t mistake it for steady: it climbs from zero to the peak value and tumbles back twice every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — bullies it into keeping one sign — but does nothing whatever to smooth it.
mack.18: The same split-ring commutator turns up in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, doing precisely the opposite job: in the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution so the torque keeps pushing the same way round; in the generator it reverses the coil’s connection to the circuit so the output keeps one polarity. And remember — a generator’s rotation comes from the external torque, not from its commutator.

mack.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not give you a constant voltage. Its output still rises and falls continuously between zero and the peak value, twice per revolution — it is DC only in the sense that the sign never reverses. And describing the commutator’s job in a generator as “keeping the coil turning” smuggles the motor explanation into the wrong machine entirely.
mack.21: Put an oscilloscope across the output terminals and it draws you the emf–time trace, and four quantities describe it. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the highest value the emf ever reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full vertical span from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest, so Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — for a single rotating coil, exactly one revolution. The frequency f is the number of cycles each second:
mack.22: When the alternator drives a current, that current alternates in lockstep with the emf and is read off the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 lines up one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs with the trace measurements marked. Reading it off is direct: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — the coil turns 25 times each second.
mack.23: Now picture the coil driven twice as fast. Two quantities shift together. Each revolution takes half as long, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. mack.24: The reverse reading matters just as much: a trace whose amplitude has doubled while the period stays put cannot be the work of faster rotation, which would have shortened the period too. An amplitude-only change has to come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A — each one scales the flux swing without laying a finger on the timing.
