czerski.25: Key idea Spin speed sets both the timing and the size of the output: double it and you double the frequency and the amplitude together. An amplitude change with the frequency left alone has to come from N, B or A instead.
czerski.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell makes electricity with nothing moving at all: it turns light energy straight into electrical energy. No coil, no magnet, no changing flux anywhere in sight. Light soaked up in thin layers of treated semiconductor knocks electrons loose, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers herds them around the external circuit in one direction only: the output is DC for as long as light keeps falling on the cell. A single cell only musters a small voltage, so a solar panel strings many cells together, and a rooftop array many panels.
czerski.27: Exactly how those layers set up the junction field is semiconductor physics beyond this course, and the inverter’s internal wiring is left out for the same reason; all you need is the input–output behaviour of each device.
czerski.28: Households run on the grid’s standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz — alternating, because the grid was built around alternators in the first place, and because transformers only work on AC. So a rooftop solar system feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that converts the panels’ DC into mains-compatible AC, which can then run the household’s appliances or be pushed back out onto the grid.
czerski.29: Connects Light can knock electrons free from a material because it hands over its energy in discrete packets — photons, as treated in Unit 4. The photoelectric effect is the laboratory demonstration of the very physics every PV cell leans on.

veritasium.0: Turn a coil steadily in a constant magnetic field and the flux through it never stops changing — which means an emf keeps getting induced for as long as you keep cranking. Everything else is just wiring. The one design choice left is how you connect that spinning coil to the outside world: slip rings hand you AC, while a split-ring commutator hands you DC.
veritasium.1: A generator is a coil of N turns sitting on an axle between the poles of a magnet, spun by some external torque — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, or just your hand on a crank. Here's the thing: it's the exact same simple DC motor from Area of Study 2, only with the energy flowing the other way. A motor takes electrical energy and gives you rotation; a generator takes rotation and gives you electrical energy. Same machine, reversed. Figure 7.1 shows the two standard forms.
veritasium.2: As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux through it never holds still: it's greatest when the plane of the coil is perpendicular to the field and the coil shows its full face to the field lines, and it drops to zero when the plane is parallel to the field and not a single line threads through. In the maximum-flux position:
veritasium.3: Spin the coil at a steady rate and it slides smoothly between these two extremes, so the flux rises and falls sinusoidally between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. Faraday’s law takes that changing flux and turns it into an emf:
veritasium.4: Here's where your intuition leads you astray. The emf at any instant isn't set by how much flux there is — it's set by the gradient of the flux–time graph, how fast the flux is changing. So two positions in every rotation do the exact opposite of what you'd guess from the size of the flux. When the flux is at a maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — you might expect the biggest emf, but the flux is momentarily frozen, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, so the emf is zero. And when the flux is zero — coil plane parallel to the field — that's when the flux is changing at its absolute fastest, because the field lines are about to stop threading one face of the coil and start threading the other: the gradient is steepest, so the emf is at its peak.
veritasium.5: So the emf trace comes out as a sinusoid with the same period as the flux, but slid along by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak sits directly below a flux zero, and every emf zero below a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 lines up one full rotation against both graphs.
veritasium.6: Key idea The induced emf tracks the gradient of the flux, not its size: ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and momentarily frozen.
veritasium.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” is the relationship backwards — it's the trap almost everyone falls into. At the maximum-flux position the coil shows a full but momentarily unchanging face to the field, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero right then. What an emf needs is for the flux to be changing — it doesn't care whether the flux is large.
veritasium.8: Magnus hand-cranks a generator whose rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Calculate the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil rotates from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
veritasium.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
veritasium.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, falling to zero a quarter turn later, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
veritasium.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — and here's the easy slip, it's a quarter, not the full period.
veritasium.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. Remember this is an average across the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf keeps changing all the way round, hitting its peak at the zero-flux instant.
veritasium.13: Connects This is exactly the coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor, just run in reverse. In Area of Study 2 you put a current in the field and got rotation out via F = nIlB; here you put rotation in the field and get an emf out via Faraday’s law — one machine, two directions of energy flow.
veritasium.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the rotating coil joins onto its own continuous metal ring — a slip ring — mounted on the axle, and a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, as shown in Figure 7.1(a). You might wonder why the connecting wires don't just twist themselves into a knot as the coil spins — that's the whole point of the slip rings: they keep continuous electrical contact between the rotating coil and the stationary external circuit, so current can be drawn off without anything winding up.
veritasium.15: Because each coil end keeps its own ring forever, the connections never swap, and the terminals copy the coil’s emf exactly: when the emf flips every half revolution, the output flips right along with it, so the alternator hands you the untouched sinusoid — alternating current. This is how almost all the electricity in the grid gets made, by turbine-driven alternators spinning at a rate locked to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. On any diagram, the giveaway is the count: two separate rings means slip rings, and that means AC output.
veritasium.16: Now swap the two slip rings for a single ring split into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half wired to one end of the coil — and the very same machine hands you DC instead, as shown in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and jump from one half-ring to the other, timed precisely to the moment the coil passes the maximum-flux position — the instant the emf is zero and just about to reverse. So each time the coil’s emf flips, the connection flips with it, and the polarity at the output terminals never changes: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid get folded upward, as shown in Figure 7.2.
veritasium.17: The output is direct current — but don't picture a flat line. It still climbs from zero to the peak and falls back twice every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — it forces it to keep one sign — but it does nothing whatsoever to smooth it.
veritasium.18: That same split-ring commutator shows up in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, doing the mirror-image job. In the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution so the torque keeps pushing one way; in the generator it reverses the coil’s connection to the circuit so the output keeps one polarity. And don't be fooled into thinking the commutator does the spinning — a generator’s rotation comes from the external torque, never from its commutator.
veritasium.19: Key idea One component, two jobs: the split-ring commutator reverses the current through a motor’s coil, and rectifies the output of a generator. Slip rings preserve AC; a split ring produces varying DC.
veritasium.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not give you a constant voltage, however much it looks like it should. Its output still swings continuously between zero and the peak, twice per revolution — it's DC only in the sense that the sign never flips. And describing the commutator’s job in a generator as “keeping the coil turning” smuggles the motor explanation into the wrong machine.
veritasium.21: Put an oscilloscope across the output terminals and it draws the emf–time trace for you, and four quantities pin it down. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the highest value the emf reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full vertical span from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest, so Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — for a single rotating coil, exactly one revolution. The frequency f is the number of cycles each second:
veritasium.22: When the alternator drives a current, the current alternates in lockstep with the emf and you read it the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 sets one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs with the trace measurements marked. Reading it off is direct: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — the coil turns 25 times each second.
veritasium.23: Now spin the coil twice as fast and watch two things move together. Each revolution takes half the time, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. But here's the part people miss: every flux change now happens in half the time too. ΔΦB is unchanged while Δt is halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf doubles at every point on the trace — the amplitude jumps from 6.0 V to 12 V and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
veritasium.24: Read it the other way and it's just as telling: if you see a trace whose amplitude has doubled while the period stayed the same, that can't be faster rotation, because faster rotation would have shrunk the period too. An amplitude-only change has to come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A — each one scales the flux swing without touching the timing.
veritasium.25: Key idea Rotation speed controls both the timing and the size of the output: double it and you double both the frequency and the amplitude. An amplitude change at constant frequency has to come from N, B or A instead.
veritasium.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell makes electricity with no moving parts at all — it converts light energy straight into electrical energy. No coil, no magnet, no changing flux anywhere. Light soaked up in thin layers of treated semiconductor knocks electrons loose, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers shoves them around the external circuit in one direction only: the output is DC for as long as light keeps landing on the cell. A single cell only musters a small voltage, so a solar panel wires many cells together, and a rooftop array wires together many panels.
veritasium.27: How those layers set up that junction field is semiconductor physics that lives beyond this course, and the inverter’s internal circuitry is left out for the same reason; all you need is the input–output behaviour of each device.
veritasium.28: Households run on the grid’s standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz — alternating, because the grid was built around alternators in the first place, and because transformers only work on AC. So a rooftop solar system feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that turns the panels’ DC into mains-compatible AC, which can then run the household’s appliances or be exported back to the grid.
veritasium.29: Connects Light can knock electrons out of a material because it delivers its energy in discrete packets — photons, as treated in Unit 4. The photoelectric effect is the laboratory demonstration of the very physics every PV cell leans on.

attenborough.0: Turn a coil steadily in a constant magnetic field and the flux threading it never settles — it changes, on and on, for as long as the coil keeps turning, and an emf is induced the whole while. Only one design question remains: how shall the turning coil speak to the world beyond it? Slip rings carry its message as AC; a split-ring commutator carries it as DC.
attenborough.1: A generator is a coil of N turns, mounted upon an axle between the poles of a magnet, and set spinning by some outside hand — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, or simply a crank turned by a person. It is, in truth, the humble DC motor of Area of Study 2, with the flow of energy quietly reversed: where a motor takes electrical energy and gives back rotation, the generator takes rotation and gives back electrical energy. Figure 7.1 shows the two forms it may take.
attenborough.2: As the coil turns, the magnetic flux through it is forever shifting: greatest when the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field and offers its full face to the field lines, and falling to nothing when the plane lies parallel to the field and not a single line threads through. In that position of greatest flux:
attenborough.3: Steady rotation carries the coil gently between these two attitudes, and so the flux swells and ebbs, sinusoidally, between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. And Faraday’s law takes that ever-changing flux and turns it into an emf:
attenborough.4: The emf, at any instant, is set not by how large the flux is, but by the gradient of the flux–time graph — how steeply it is changing. And so two moments in every turn behave in just the opposite way to what one might first suppose. When the flux stands at its maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — it is, for that fleeting instant, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, and the emf with it. But when the flux falls to zero — coil plane parallel to the field — it is changing at its very fastest, for the field lines are on the point of abandoning one face of the coil and threading the other: the gradient is steepest, and the emf reaches its peak.
attenborough.5: The emf trace, then, is a sinusoid — the same period as the flux, but shifted by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak sits directly beneath a flux zero, and every emf zero beneath a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 sets one full rotation against both graphs, that you may see them together.
attenborough.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and momentarily unchanging.
attenborough.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” has the relationship turned exactly the wrong way round. At the position of greatest flux the coil offers its full face to the field, yes — but for that instant the flux is utterly still, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf falls to zero. An emf asks only that the flux be changing — never that it be large.
attenborough.8: Magnus turns the crank of a generator by hand. Its rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, set within a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, and he holds it to a steady 10 Hz. Find the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil swings from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
attenborough.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
attenborough.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the position of greatest flux, falling away to zero a quarter turn later, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
attenborough.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a single quarter turn takes only Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — not the whole period.
attenborough.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. This is an average, taken across the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf rises and falls throughout the rotation, peaking at the very instant the flux is zero.
attenborough.13: Connects This is the coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor run in reverse. In Area of Study 2 a current set within the field produced rotation, by F = nIlB; here rotation within the field produces an emf, by Faraday’s law — one machine, and two directions in which its energy may flow.
attenborough.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the turning coil is joined to its own unbroken metal ring — a slip ring — riding upon the axle, and a still carbon brush rests gently against each ring, as shown in Figure 7.1(a). The slip rings keep faithful, unbroken contact between the turning coil and the still circuit beyond, so current may be drawn away without the connecting wires ever twisting upon themselves.
attenborough.15: Because each end of the coil keeps its own ring, always and forever, the connections never swap, and the terminals follow the coil’s emf exactly: when the emf reverses, every half revolution, the output reverses faithfully with it, and the alternator delivers the sinusoid untouched — alternating current. Almost all the electricity of the grid is born this way, from turbine-driven alternators turning at a rate held in step with the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. And on any diagram, the cue that gives it away is simply the count: two separate rings means slip rings, and so AC output.
attenborough.16: Now replace those two slip rings with a single ring, cleft into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half joined to one end of the coil — and the very same machine delivers DC, as shown in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and pass from one half-ring to the other, timed to the exact instant the coil passes the maximum-flux position — the moment the emf is zero and poised to reverse. Each time the coil’s emf turns about, the connections turn about with it, so the polarity at the output terminals never changes: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid are folded upward, as shown in Figure 7.2.
attenborough.17: The output is direct current — yet it knows no rest: it climbs from zero to its peak and falls away again, twice in every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — compels it to hold one sign — but it does nothing whatever to smooth it.
attenborough.18: That same split-ring commutator appears in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, where it does the opposite work: in the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution, so the torque holds to one direction of turning; in the generator it reverses the coil’s connection to the circuit, so the output holds to one polarity. A generator’s rotation is given to it by the outside torque — never by its commutator.
attenborough.19: Key idea One component, two roles: the split-ring commutator reverses the current through a motor’s coil, and rectifies the output of a generator. Slip rings preserve AC; a split ring produces varying DC.
attenborough.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not produce a constant voltage. Its output still rises and falls, ceaselessly, between zero and the peak value, twice in every revolution — it is DC only in the sense that its sign never reverses. And to describe the commutator’s task in a generator as “keeping the coil turning” is to carry the motor’s story into the wrong machine altogether.
attenborough.21: An oscilloscope, laid across the output terminals, draws out the emf–time trace, and four quantities describe what it shows. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the greatest height the emf ever reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full vertical span, from the floor of a trough to the crown of a crest, so that Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — for a single turning coil, exactly one revolution. And the frequency f is the number of cycles in each second:
attenborough.22: When the alternator drives a current, that current alternates in step with the emf, and is read in just the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 sets one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs, with the trace measurements marked. The reading is direct: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — the coil turns 25 times in every second.
attenborough.23: Now picture the coil driven twice as fast. Two quantities shift together. Each revolution takes half the time, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. And in the same breath, every flux change now happens in half the time: ΔΦB is unchanged while Δt is halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf doubles at every point along the trace — the amplitude climbs from 6.0 V to 12 V, and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
attenborough.24: The reverse reading matters just as much: a trace whose amplitude has doubled while its period stays the same cannot have come from faster rotation, for that would also have shortened the period. A change in amplitude alone must come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A — each one scales the swing of the flux without ever touching the timing.
attenborough.25: Key idea Rotation speed sets both the timing and the size of the output: doubling it doubles both the frequency and the amplitude. An amplitude change at constant frequency must come from N, B or A instead.
attenborough.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell makes electricity with no motion at all: it takes light energy and turns it directly into electrical energy. There is no coil here, no magnet, no changing flux — nothing turns. Light, drunk in by thin layers of treated semiconductor, frees electrons, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers drives them around the external circuit in one direction only: the output is DC for as long as light falls upon the cell. A single cell yields but a small voltage, and so a solar panel gathers many cells together, and a rooftop array many panels.
attenborough.27: How the layers come to hold that junction field is a matter of semiconductor physics, lying beyond the bounds of this course, and the inverter’s inner circuitry is set aside for the very same reason; only the input–output behaviour of each device is asked of you.
attenborough.28: Households live upon the grid’s standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz — alternating, because the grid was raised around alternators, and because transformers will work on nothing but AC. A rooftop solar system therefore feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that converts the panels’ DC into mains-compatible AC, which may then power the household’s appliances, or be sent out to the grid.

greekepic.0: Turn a coil steadily within an unchanging magnetic field, and the flux threading it will not rest but change without ceasing, so that an emf is begotten for as long as the coil keeps its turning. One choice alone remains to the maker: how the wheeling coil shall be joined to the world beyond it. Slip rings pour forth AC; a split-ring commutator pours forth DC.
greekepic.1: A generator is a coil of N turns, set upon an axle between the poles of a magnet and driven round by some torque from without — the breath of a steam turbine, the thrust of a wind turbine, or the labour of a turning hand. It is the humble DC motor of Area of Study 2, with the river of energy reversed in its flow: a motor takes electrical energy and begets rotation; a generator takes rotation and begets electrical energy. Figure 7.1 reveals the two appointed forms.
greekepic.2: As the coil wheels about, the magnetic flux that threads it changes without pause: greatest when the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field and the coil offers its whole face to the marching field lines, and nothing at all when the plane lies parallel to the field and no line of force threads it through. In the place of greatest flux:
greekepic.3: Steady rotation bears the coil smoothly between these stations, so the flux rises and falls in sinusoidal song between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. And Faraday's law, that ancient decree, turns this shifting flux into an emf:
greekepic.4: The emf at any instant is ruled by the gradient of the flux–time graph, and never by the size of the flux itself. Two stations in every turning therefore behave against the first reckoning of the unwary, who would judge by the flux's size. When the flux stands at its height — coil plane perpendicular to the field — the flux is for that moment not changing at all: the gradient is zero, and so the emf is zero. When the flux falls to nothing — coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing at its swiftest, for the field lines are about to forsake one face of the coil and take up the other: the gradient is steepest, and so the emf reaches its peak.
greekepic.5: The emf trace is therefore a sinusoid, sharing the very period of the flux yet shifted by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak rests directly beneath a flux zero, and every emf zero beneath a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 sets one full revolution against both graphs.
greekepic.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε rises to a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing most swiftly, and ε falls to zero where ΦB is a maximum and for that moment unchanging.
greekepic.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” speaks the decree backwards, and so deceives. At the place of greatest flux the coil offers a full but momentarily unchanging face to the field, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is nothing in that instant. An emf demands that the flux be changing — it does not demand that the flux be great.
greekepic.8: Magnus turns by hand a generator whose rectangular coil bears 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, set in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Reckon the magnitude of the average emf begotten as the coil wheels from the position of greatest flux to the position of no flux.
greekepic.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the whole change of flux and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
greekepic.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the position of greatest flux, falling to nothing a quarter turn after, so that ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
greekepic.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn claims Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — not the whole period, mark it well.
greekepic.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. This is an average taken across the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf shifts throughout the revolution, climbing to its peak at the very instant of no flux.

In Area of Study 2 a current laid within the field begot rotation by F = nIlB; here rotation within the field begets an emf by Faraday's law — one machine, and two directions in which its energy may flow.
greekepic.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the wheeling coil is wedded to its own unbroken ring of metal — a slip ring — mounted upon the axle, and a still carbon brush presses against each ring, as Figure 7.1(a) shows. The slip rings keep unbroken the bond of contact between the turning coil and the still circuit beyond, so that current may be drawn from the coil without the connecting wires ever twisting upon themselves.
greekepic.15: Because each end of the coil keeps its own ring for ever, the connections never trade places, and the terminals follow the coil's emf with perfect faith: when the emf reverses with every half revolution, the output reverses alongside it, and the alternator pours forth the unaltered sinusoid — alternating current. Nearly all the power of the grid is begotten thus, by turbine-driven alternators turning at a pace bound fast to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. greekepic.16: Take away the two slip rings and set in their place a single ring cleft into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half joined to one end of the coil — and the very same machine pours forth DC, as Figure 7.1(b) shows. With every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and pass from one half-ring to the other, timed to the very instant the coil passes the position of greatest flux — the moment the emf is zero and poised to reverse its direction. Each time the coil's emf reverses, the connections reverse alongside it, so the polarity at the output terminals never alters: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid are folded upward, as Figure 7.2 shows.

greekepic.22: When the alternator drives a current, the current alternates in step with the emf and is read in the selfsame manner: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 sets one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs, with the trace measurements marked. The reading is plain: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — the coil turns 25 times in every second.
greekepic.23: Consider now the coil driven twice as swiftly. Two quantities shift together. Each revolution claims half the time, so the period is halved to 20 ms and the frequency is doubled to 50 Hz. And at once, every change of flux now comes to pass in half the time: ΔΦB stands unchanged while Δt is halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf is doubled at every point upon the trace — the amplitude climbs from 6.0 V to 12 V, and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
greekepic.24: The reverse reading bears equal weight: a trace whose amplitude has doubled at an unchanged period cannot have sprung from swifter rotation, which would also have shortened the period. A change in amplitude alone must come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B, or the coil area A — each scales the swing of the flux without ever touching the timing.
greekepic.25: Key idea Rotation speed rules both the timing and the size of the output: to double it is to double both the frequency and the amplitude alike. A change in amplitude at unchanging frequency must come instead from N, B or A.
greekepic.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell begets electricity with no motion at all: it turns light energy straightway into electrical energy. There is no coil, no magnet, no changing flux. Light drunk down within thin layers of treated semiconductor frees electrons, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers drives them round the external circuit in one direction only: the output is DC for as long as light falls upon the cell. A single cell yields but a small voltage, so a solar panel joins many cells together, and a rooftop array many panels.
greekepic.27: How the layers raise up that junction field is the lore of semiconductors, beyond the bounds of this course, and the inverter's inner circuitry is barred for the selfsame reason; only the input–output behaviour of each device is asked of you.
greekepic.28: Households run upon the grid's appointed supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz — alternating, because the grid was raised about alternators, and because transformers will labour upon AC alone. A rooftop solar system therefore feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that turns the panels' DC into mains-fit AC, which may then power the household's appliances or be sent forth to the grid.
greekepic.29: Connects Light has the power to free electrons from a material because it bears its energy in discrete packets — photons, as told in Unit 4. The photoelectric effect is the laboratory showing-forth of the very physics upon which every PV cell relies.

cnpop.0: 注意看，让一个线圈在恒定磁场里匀速转起来，穿过它的磁通量就会一直变来变去，于是只要线圈不停转，感应电动势就一直在线供应。剩下唯一要拍板的设计问题是：转动的线圈怎么跟外电路接上？接滑环，出来的就是交流；接换向器（分裂环），出来的就是直流。
cnpop.1: 发电机就是一个有 N 匝的线圈，架在磁铁两极之间的转轴上，再靠外部力矩——比如蒸汽轮机、风力涡轮，或者干脆一只手摇把——把它转起来。它其实就是 Area of Study 2 里那台简单直流电动机，只不过能量转换的方向被反过来了：电动机把电能变成转动，发电机把转动变成电能。图 7.1 展示了两种标准形式。
cnpop.2: 线圈一转，穿过它的磁通量就连续地变：当线圈平面跟磁场垂直、整个面正对着磁感线时，磁通量最大；当线圈平面跟磁场平行、一根磁感线都穿不过去时，磁通量为零。在磁通量最大的位置上：
cnpop.3: 匀速转动让线圈在这两种姿态之间丝滑切换，所以磁通量就在 +B⊥A 和 −B⊥A 之间正弦式地起起落落。法拉第定律一出手，就把这个变化的磁通量变成了电动势：
cnpop.4: 注意看，任何一瞬间的电动势，是由磁通—时间图的斜率说了算的，跟磁通量本身有多大根本没关系。所以每转一圈，有两个位置的表现，恰恰跟你一开始凭“磁通量大小”脑补出来的反着来。当磁通量最大时——线圈平面垂直于磁场——磁通量在这一刻压根没在变：斜率为零，电动势也为零。当磁通量为零时——线圈平面平行于磁场——磁通量变得飞快，因为磁感线正要从线圈的一个面撤走、转头穿向另一个面：斜率最陡，电动势也冲到峰值。
cnpop.5: 所以电动势的曲线也是一条正弦线，周期跟磁通量一模一样，但整体平移了四分之一个周期（90°）：每一个电动势峰值都稳稳坐落在某个磁通量零点的正下方，每一个电动势零点都对着一个磁通量最大值。图 7.3 把一整圈转动跟这两条曲线摆在一起对照。
cnpop.6: Key idea 感应电动势跟着磁通量的斜率走：当 ΦB 为零、变化最快时 ε 最大；当 ΦB 最大、这一刻又纹丝不动时 ε 为零。
cnpop.7: Trap 划重点别踩坑：“磁通量最大就给出最大电动势”——这句话把关系彻底说反了。在磁通量最大的位置，线圈正对着磁场亮出整个满面，可这一刻它偏偏不变，所以 ΔΦB/Δt = 0，那一瞬间电动势为零。电动势要的是磁通量在变——它从来不要求磁通量有多大，记牢了。
cnpop.8: Magnus 手摇一台发电机，它的矩形线圈有 50 匝，每匝 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm，处在 0.25 T 的匀强磁场里，稳稳地以 10 Hz 转动。求线圈从磁通量最大位置转到磁通量为零位置时，所感应出的平均电动势的大小。
cnpop.9: 公式：平均电动势的大小是 εavg = N ΔΦBΔt，其中 ΔΦB 是磁通量的完整变化量，Δt 是转过四分之一圈所用的时间。
cnpop.10: 磁通量变化：在磁通量最大位置 ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb，再转四分之一圈后掉到零，所以 ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb。
cnpop.11: 时间：T = 1/f = 0.10 s，转四分之一圈只花 Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s——可别填成一整个周期。
cnpop.12: 答案：εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V。这是整段四分之一圈里的平均值；真到了转动过程里，瞬时电动势是一路变的，在磁通量为零那一瞬冲到峰值。
cnpop.13: Connects 这就是 那台简单直流电动机 的“线圈在磁场里”几何结构，倒着跑一遍。在 Area of Study 2 里，把电流丢进磁场，靠 F = nIlB 产生转动；这里反过来，线圈在磁场里转动，靠法拉第定律产生电动势——同一台机器，两个能量流动方向。
cnpop.14: 交流发电机（alternator） 就是一台 AC 发电机。转动线圈的每一端，各自连到自己专属的一整圈金属环——叫 滑环（slip ring）——装在转轴上，再各有一块固定不动的碳刷死死压着，如图 7.1(a) 所示。滑环负责在转动的线圈和固定的外电路之间维持不间断的电接触，这样就能从线圈里取电，又不至于把连接的导线越拧越死。
cnpop.15: 因为每个线圈端永远咬着自己那只环，接法从不互换，端子就老老实实地完全跟着线圈的电动势走：每转半圈电动势翻一次向，输出也跟着翻，于是交流发电机原汁原味地交出那条正弦曲线——也就是交流电。几乎所有电网里的电都是这么发出来的，靠涡轮带动的交流发电机，转速死死锁定在澳大利亚市电的 50 Hz 上。在任何图上，认它的暗号就是数环子：两只分开的环，就是滑环，那就一定是 AC 输出。
cnpop.16: 把那两只滑环换成一整只环、再从中间劈成两片相互绝缘的半环——这就是 分裂环换向器（split-ring commutator），每一片各连线圈的一端——同一台机器交出来的就成了 DC，如图 7.1(b) 所示。每转半圈，碳刷恰好扫过缝隙，从一片半环跳到另一片，而且时机卡得极准，正好赶在线圈经过磁通量最大位置那一刻——也就是电动势为零、正要翻向的瞬间。每当线圈的电动势翻向，接法也跟着翻，所以输出端子的极性永远不变：正弦曲线那些负的半周期，被整个翻到上面去了，如图 7.2 所示。
cnpop.17: 输出是直流，但它可不平稳：它每转一圈，会两次从零升到峰值、再落回零。换向器干的是整流（rectify）——硬逼着输出只保留一个符号——可它对“抹平”波动这件事啥都没干。
cnpop.18: 同样这只分裂环换向器，也出现在 Area of Study 2 的直流电动机里，干的却是反着的活：在电动机里，它每转半圈就把供给电流在线圈里翻一次向，好让力矩始终朝同一个旋转方向；在发电机里，它每转半圈就把线圈到电路的接法翻一次向，好让输出始终保持同一个极性。发电机的转动是外部力矩造出来的，可不是换向器造出来的。
cnpop.19: Key idea 一个零件，两副面孔：分裂环换向器在电动机里翻转流过线圈的电流，在发电机里整流它的输出。滑环保住 AC；分裂环则产出起伏不定的 DC。
cnpop.20: Trap 划重点别踩坑：分裂环换向器并不会输出一个恒定电压。它的输出照样每转一圈两次、在零和峰值之间连续起伏——它之所以叫 DC，仅仅是因为符号从不翻向而已。要是把换向器在发电机里的活儿描述成“让线圈一直转下去”，那是把电动机的解释硬塞进了错的机器里，可别张冠李戴。
cnpop.21: 在输出端子上接一台示波器，它会画出电动势—时间曲线，有四个量来描述它。振幅（amplitude） 就是峰值电压 Vpeak，电动势能冲到的最大值。峰峰值电压 Vp–p 是从波谷底到波峰顶的完整竖直跨度，所以对正弦波来说 Vp–p = 2Vpeak。周期 T 是走完一个完整循环的时间——对一个单独转动的线圈来说，恰好就是转一整圈。频率 f 是每秒钟跑完的循环数：
cnpop.22: 当交流发电机驱动出电流时，电流跟电动势齐步走，读法也一模一样：峰值电流 Ipeak，峰峰值电流 Ip–p = 2Ipeak。图 7.3 把线圈转一整圈跟磁通量、电动势曲线摆在一起对照，曲线上的测量值也都标好了。读起来很直接：波峰在 +6.0 V、波谷在 −6.0 V，相邻两个波峰相隔 40 ms，于是振幅是 6.0 V，Vp–p = 12 V，T = 0.040 s，f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz——也就是线圈每秒转 25 圈。
cnpop.23: 现在把线圈的转速翻一倍来看看。有两个量会手拉手一起变。每转一圈只花一半时间，所以周期减半到 20 ms，频率翻倍到 50 Hz。与此同时，每一次磁通量变化现在都在一半的时间里完成：ΔΦB 没变，Δt 却减半了，于是由 ε = −NΔΦB/Δt 可知，曲线上每一点的电动势都翻倍——振幅从 6.0 V 涨到 12 V，Vp–p 从 12 V 涨到 24 V。
cnpop.24: 反过来读同样关键：要是一条曲线振幅翻倍了、周期却没动，那它绝不可能是转得更快造成的，因为转得更快会把周期也缩短。一个“只动振幅、不动周期”的变化，只能来自把匝数 N、磁场强度 B 或线圈面积 A 翻倍——它们各自只放大磁通量的摆幅，却碰都不碰时间。
cnpop.25: Key idea 转速同时管着输出的时间和大小：把它翻倍，频率和振幅就一起翻倍。而一个频率不变、只变振幅的变化，就只能来自 N、B 或 A 了。
cnpop.26: 光伏（PV）电池 发起电来压根不需要任何转动：它直接把光能变成电能。没有线圈，没有磁铁，也没有变化的磁通量。光被处理过的半导体薄层吸收后，把电子解放出来，而两层交界处那个自带的内建电场，会驱着这些电子单向地绕外电路跑：只要光还照在电池上，输出就是 DC。单独一个电池只能产出一丢丢电压，所以一块太阳能板把许多电池连起来，屋顶上一整阵列又把许多板连起来。
cnpop.27: 那些薄层到底怎么建立起这个结电场，属于本课程范围之外的半导体物理；逆变器内部那套电路同理也被排除在外；要掌握的只是每个器件的输入—输出行为而已。
cnpop.28: 家家户户都跑在电网的标准供电上：230 V rms 的 AC，50 Hz——之所以是交流，一是因为电网当初就是围着交流发电机建起来的，二是因为变压器只对 AC 才管用。所以一套屋顶太阳能系统会把它的电池板喂进一台 逆变器（inverter），这家伙专门把电池板那点 DC 变成跟市电兼容的 AC，然后就能拿去供家里电器用，或者卖回电网。
cnpop.29: Connects 光之所以能把电子从材料里解放出来，是因为它把能量打包成一颗颗离散的小份送过来——也就是光子，正如 Unit 4 里讲的那样。光电效应 就是实验室里那场现身说法，每一片 PV 电池都靠它背后这套物理吃饭。

ai.0: Spin a coil steadily inside a constant magnetic field and the flux threading it never stops changing — so an emf keeps getting induced for as long as you keep it turning. The only real design decision left is how you wire that spinning coil to the outside world: slip rings hand you AC; a split-ring commutator hands you DC.
ai.1: A generator is a coil of N turns mounted on an axle between the poles of a magnet, spun by some outside torque — a steam turbine, a wind turbine, or just your own arm on a hand crank. It is the simple DC motor you met in Area of Study 2, but with the direction of energy flow flipped: a motor turns electrical energy into rotation; a generator turns rotation back into electrical energy. Figure 7.1 shows the two standard forms.
ai.2: As the coil turns, the magnetic flux through it shifts continuously: it is greatest when the plane of the coil sits perpendicular to the field and the coil shows its full face to the field lines, and it drops to zero when the plane lies parallel to the field and not a single field line threads through. In that maximum-flux position:
ai.3: Steady rotation slides the coil smoothly between these two orientations, so the flux swells and shrinks sinusoidally between +B⊥A and −B⊥A. Then Faraday’s law takes that changing flux and turns it into an emf:
ai.4: Here’s the subtle part: the emf at any instant is set by the gradient of the flux–time graph, not by how big the flux happens to be. So two positions in each rotation behave exactly opposite to what you’d first guess from the size of the flux. When the flux is at a maximum — coil plane perpendicular to the field — the flux is, just for that instant, not changing at all: the gradient is zero, so the emf is zero too. And when the flux is zero — coil plane parallel to the field — the flux is changing at its absolute fastest, because the field lines are about to stop threading one face of the coil and start threading the other: the gradient is steepest, so the emf hits its peak.
ai.5: The emf trace, then, is a sinusoid with the same period as the flux but shoved along by a quarter of a cycle (90°): every emf peak sits directly beneath a flux zero, and every emf zero beneath a flux maximum. Figure 7.3 lines up one full rotation against both graphs so you can see it for yourself.
ai.6: Key idea The induced emf follows the gradient of the flux: ε is a maximum where ΦB is zero and changing fastest, and ε is zero where ΦB is a maximum and momentarily frozen.
ai.7: Trap “Maximum flux gives maximum emf” has the relationship back to front. At the maximum-flux position the coil shows a full but momentarily unchanging face to the field, so ΔΦB/Δt = 0 and the emf is zero at that instant. An emf needs the flux to be changing — it does not care one bit whether the flux is large.
ai.8: Magnus hand-cranks a generator whose rectangular coil has 50 turns, each 8.0 cm × 5.0 cm, in a uniform magnetic field of 0.25 T, at a steady 10 Hz. Calculate the magnitude of the average emf induced as the coil swings from the maximum-flux position to the zero-flux position.
ai.9: Formula: the magnitude of the average emf is εavg = N ΔΦBΔt, where ΔΦB is the full flux change and Δt is the time for one quarter of a revolution.
ai.10: Flux change: ΦB = B⊥A = 0.25 × (0.080 × 0.050) = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb at the maximum-flux position, dropping to zero a quarter turn later, so ΔΦB = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
ai.11: Time: T = 1/f = 0.10 s, and a quarter turn takes Δt = T/4 = 0.025 s — not the full period, so watch that you don’t reach for T by mistake.
ai.12: Answer: εavg = 50 × 1.0 × 10⁻³ ÷ 0.025 = 2.0 V. Remember this is an average over the quarter turn; the instantaneous emf varies right through the rotation, peaking at the zero-flux instant.
ai.13: Connects This is exactly the coil-in-field geometry of the simple DC motor run backwards. In Area of Study 2 a current dropped into the field produced rotation via F = nIlB; here rotation in the field produces an emf via Faraday’s law — one machine, two directions of energy flow.
ai.14: An alternator is an AC generator. Each end of the spinning coil is joined to its own continuous metal ring — a slip ring — mounted on the axle, and a stationary carbon brush presses against each ring, as shown in Figure 7.1(a). The slip rings keep electrical contact flowing between the rotating coil and the stationary external circuit, so you can draw current off the coil without the connecting wires twisting themselves into knots.
ai.15: Because each coil end hangs onto its own ring permanently, the connections never swap, and the terminals track the coil’s emf exactly: when the emf reverses every half revolution, the output reverses right along with it, and the alternator delivers the sinusoid untouched — alternating current. Almost all of the grid’s electricity is made this way, by turbine-driven alternators spinning at a rate locked to the 50 Hz of the Australian mains. On any diagram, the giveaway is simply the count: two separate rings means slip rings, and therefore AC output.
ai.16: Now swap the two slip rings for a single ring split into two insulated halves — a split-ring commutator, each half wired to one end of the coil — and the very same machine hands you DC instead, as shown in Figure 7.1(b). Every half revolution the brushes cross the gaps and jump from one half-ring to the other, timed precisely to the coil passing the maximum-flux position — the instant the emf is zero and just about to reverse. So each time the coil’s emf flips, the connections flip with it, and the polarity at the output terminals never changes: the negative half-cycles of the sinusoid get folded upward, as shown in Figure 7.2.
ai.17: The output is direct current, but don’t mistake it for steady: it climbs from zero up to the peak and tumbles back down twice every revolution. The commutator rectifies the output — forces it to keep one sign — but does nothing whatsoever to smooth it.
ai.18: That same split-ring commutator turns up in the DC motor of Area of Study 2, doing the opposite job: in the motor it reverses the supply current through the coil every half revolution so the torque keeps pushing in one rotational direction; in the generator it reverses the coil’s connection to the circuit so the output keeps one polarity. And remember — a generator’s rotation comes from the external torque, never from its commutator.
ai.19: Key idea One component, two jobs: the split-ring commutator reverses the current through a motor’s coil, and rectifies the output of a generator. Slip rings preserve AC; a split ring produces varying DC.
ai.20: Trap A split-ring commutator does not produce a constant voltage. Its output still swings continuously between zero and the peak, twice per revolution — it is DC only in the sense that the sign never flips. And describing the commutator’s job in a generator as “keeping the coil turning” smuggles the motor explanation into the wrong machine.
ai.21: Put an oscilloscope across the output terminals and it draws the emf–time trace, with four quantities to describe it. The amplitude is the peak voltage Vpeak, the largest value the emf ever reaches. The peak-to-peak voltage Vp–p is the full vertical span from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest, so Vp–p = 2Vpeak for a sinusoid. The period T is the time for one complete cycle — for a single rotating coil, that is exactly one revolution. And the frequency f is the number of cycles packed into each second:
ai.22: When the alternator drives a current, that current alternates in step with the emf and you read it the same way: peak current Ipeak and peak-to-peak current Ip–p = 2Ipeak. Figure 7.3 sets one revolution of the coil against the flux and emf graphs with the trace measurements marked. Reading it off is direct: crests at +6.0 V and troughs at −6.0 V, with successive crests 40 ms apart, give an amplitude of 6.0 V, Vp–p = 12 V, T = 0.040 s and f = 1 ÷ 0.040 = 25 Hz — the coil spins 25 times each second.
ai.23: Now picture the coil driven twice as fast. Two quantities shift together. Each revolution takes half the time, so the period halves to 20 ms and the frequency doubles to 50 Hz. At the same time, every flux change now happens in half the time: ΔΦB stays the same while Δt is halved, so by ε = −NΔΦB/Δt the emf doubles at every point on the trace — the amplitude climbs from 6.0 V to 12 V and Vp–p from 12 V to 24 V.
ai.24: Reading it the other way round matters just as much: a trace whose amplitude has doubled while the period stays put cannot have come from faster rotation, which would also have shortened the period. An amplitude-only change has to come from doubling the number of turns N, the field strength B or the coil area A — each one scales the flux swing without touching the timing.
ai.25: Key idea Rotation speed sets both the timing and the size of the output: double it and you double both the frequency and the amplitude. An amplitude change at constant frequency has to come from N, B or A instead.
ai.26: A photovoltaic (PV) cell generates electricity with no motion at all: it converts light energy straight into electrical energy. There is no coil, no magnet, no changing flux anywhere. Light soaked up in thin layers of treated semiconductor frees electrons, and a built-in electric field at the junction between the layers shepherds them around the external circuit in one direction only: the output is DC for as long as light keeps falling on the cell. A single cell musters only a small voltage, so a solar panel wires many cells together, and a rooftop array many panels.
ai.27: How those layers set up the junction field is semiconductor physics that lies beyond this course, and the inverter’s internal circuitry is left out for the same reason; all you need is the input–output behaviour of each device.
ai.28: Households run on the grid’s standard supply: 230 V rms AC at 50 Hz — alternating, because the grid was built around alternators in the first place, and because transformers only work on AC. So a rooftop solar system feeds its panels into an inverter, the device that converts the panels’ DC into mains-compatible AC, which can then run the household’s appliances or be exported back to the grid.
ai.29: Connects Light can free electrons from a material because it delivers its energy in discrete packets — photons, as you’ll see in Unit 4. The photoelectric effect is the laboratory demonstration of the very physics every PV cell leans on.
